incident_title: GBU-57 MOP Strike: Fordow Nuclear Facility
summary: Seven B-2 Spirit stealth bombers flew round-trip from Diego Garcia carrying GBU-57 Massive Ordnance Penetrators — the only weapon capable of penetrating Fordow's deep underground bunker buried ~80–90m inside a mountain near Qom. 52 tanker aircraft required for aerial refueling support. Twelve MOPs required vs. two at Natanz reflecting extreme hardening. Iran pre-evacuated 440.9 kg enriched uranium including 408.9 kg at 60%-enriched (~9 weapons worth).
assessment: Fordow was specifically designed to survive conventional bunker-busters. The GBU-57 MOP was developed precisely for this mission. The pre-strike evacuation of uranium indicates Iran had advance warning or a prepared contingency. NBC News later assessed only Fordow was definitively destroyed of the three primary nuclear targets.
result_outcome: Facility destroyed
key_intelligence_notes: 440.9 kg uranium evacuated pre-strike incl. 408.9 kg 60%-enriched (~9 weapons worth); 52 tanker aircraft required; first MOP combat use; NBC News assessed only Fordow definitively destroyed
osint_sources: Oliver Alexander (OSINT) — F-22 deployment 4-day warning|https://twitter.com/OAlexanderDK; MATA — 100+ tankers 200+ cargo tracked|https://twitter.com/MT_Anderson
press_sources: The War Zone|https://www.thedrive.com/the-war-zone; Air & Space Forces Magazine|https://www.airandspaceforces.com; CNN (satellite imagery)|https://www.cnn.com
tags: MOP FIRST USE; NUCLEAR STRIKE; B-2 SPIRIT; GBU-57; DIEGO GARCIA

incident_title: GBU-57 Strike: Natanz Enrichment Complex
summary: Secondary MOP strike on Natanz. Two MOPs deployed vs. 12 at Fordow reflecting shallower burial depth (~8m). Natanz houses thousands of IR-1 IR-4 IR-6 centrifuges. NBC News assessed destruction as partial and effectiveness disputed.
assessment: NBC News found that of the three primary targets, only Fordow was definitively destroyed. Two MOPs vs. twelve at Fordow suggests planners underestimated the hardened underground halls.
result_outcome: Partial destruction — effectiveness disputed
key_intelligence_notes: Natanz under IAEA safeguards with cameras and monitoring
osint_sources: Oliver Alexander (OSINT)|https://twitter.com/OAlexanderDK; MATA|https://twitter.com/MT_Anderson
press_sources: NBC News (assessment: only Fordow destroyed)|https://www.nbcnews.com; The War Zone|https://www.thedrive.com/the-war-zone

tags: NATANZ; CENTRIFUGE HALLS; B-2 SPIRIT; PARTIAL EFFECT

incident_title: CCG Mass Blockade: Scarborough Shoal (Philippines)
summary: China Coast Guard deployed up to 40 vessels simultaneously to enforce de-facto blockade of Scarborough Shoal. CCG vessels used water cannons, sound devices, and laser blinding against Philippine Coast Guard ships. Prevented Filipino fishermen from accessing traditional fishing grounds inside Philippine EEZ. 2016 PCA tribunal ruled China has no legal basis for claims.
assessment: Scarborough Shoal blockade represents China's "salami slicing" gray-zone strategy — incremental coercion below the threshold of armed conflict. Each iteration of harassment normalizes the next level of coercion. The US-Philippines Mutual Defense Treaty (MDT) technically covers Philippine armed forces but has been deliberately not invoked for CCG-on-PCG harassment.
platform_or_unit: China Coast Guard (CCG) — up to 40 vessels
munitions_weapons: Water cannons; sound devices; laser dazzlers; physical blockade
result_outcome: CCG blockade sustained; Filipino fishermen excluded; FONOPS conducted by USN
economic_impact: Brent crude minimal impact; Philippine fisheries economic damage
key_intelligence_notes: 2016 PCA tribunal: China has NO legal basis for claims; blockade represents salami-slicing gray-zone strategy below armed conflict threshold; US-PHL MDT technically covers armed forces but not CCG-on-PCG harassment

incident_title: Hamas Gaza Assault: Oct 7 2023
summary: Hamas militants launched a surprise multi-pronged assault from Gaza into southern Israel on Oct 7, 2023, combining mass rocket fire with infantry infiltration via tunnels, motorcycles, and paragliders. Armed fighters overran several Israeli border towns and military posts, resulting in ~1,200 Israeli soldiers and civilians killed and ~240 taken hostage (Israeli figures). The attack included use of drones and anti-tank missiles. The breach of defenses prompted Israel to declare war and initiate a major ground and air offensive in Gaza. The operation revealed significant planning by Hamas and exposed failures in Israeli preparedness.
assessment: The Oct 7 attack exposed catastrophic failures in Israeli intelligence and border security despite decades of investment. Hamas demonstrated sophisticated multi-domain coordination — rockets, infantry, drones, tunnels — overwhelming the vaunted Gaza Division in hours. The scale of civilian casualties transformed Israeli strategic calculus from deterrence-focused to elimination-focused.
result_outcome: Hamas gained temporary control of border areas and took hostages; Israel mobilized for major retaliation

incident_title: IDF Strike: Jabalia Refugee Camp (Nov 2023)
summary: The Israeli Air Force struck Jabalia refugee camp in Gaza on Nov 1, 2023, targeting a Hamas command center inside multi-story buildings. IAF F-16I jets dropped GBU-39 small diameter bombs onto the sites. Palestinian officials said around 50 people were killed, including a senior Hamas commander. Israel said it was hitting Hamas infrastructure used for rocket launches. The high civilian toll drew condemnation from aid groups. The strike was framed as retaliation for Hamas attacks and a demonstration of Israel's intent to dismantle Hamas leadership.
assessment: The Jabalia strike exemplified the tension between precision targeting of Hamas leadership in dense urban environments and the resulting civilian toll. Use of SDB munitions in refugee camp settings drew international condemnation and fueled debates about proportionality in urban warfare.
result_outcome: Hamas infrastructure damaged; significant civilian casualties
key_intelligence_notes: Use of bunker-busting SDB in urban area; entire families reported among dead; first use of this tactic in Gaza war

incident_title: Ukrainian Assault: Sudzha (Kursk Front)
summary: Ukrainian forces launched a surprise cross-border offensive into Russia's Kursk region on Aug 7–14 2024, seizing over 1,000 sq km including the town of Sudzha. The raid involved mechanized infantry and artillery, reportedly capturing ~100 Russian POWs. Kyiv said the operation aimed to push the front westward; Russia counterattacked in late 2024 to expel the incursion. This was the first major Ukrainian operation deep in pre-2022 Russian territory, altering battle lines and spurring Moscow to vow retaliation.
assessment: Ukraine's Kursk incursion was the first significant Ukrainian ground operation inside pre-2022 Russian territory, demonstrating operational surprise capability but ultimately unsustainable without reinforcement. Russia's subsequent recapture underscored the difficulty of holding territory deep in adversary rear areas.
result_outcome: Ukrainian foothold established; largely retaken by Russia by 2025

incident_title: Ukraine Storm Shadow Strike: Kursk
summary: Ukraine fired a salvo of British Storm Shadow cruise missiles into Russia's Kursk region on Nov 20, 2024, striking deep Russian military infrastructure. Russian sources reported at least 14 large explosions near a command post. Kyiv said it targeted rear command centers to disrupt Russian operations. This followed Ukraine's first ATACMS launch a day earlier, signaling an escalation. Russia condemned the use of Western long-range missiles as a major escalation and reportedly raised its nuclear alert level.
assessment: The Storm Shadow strike marked the first combat use of UK-supplied cruise missiles against targets inside Russia. The escalation triggered Russian nuclear posture adjustments and set a precedent for Western long-range weapons employment that shaped subsequent US policy decisions.
result_outcome: Russian command post reportedly damaged; no confirmed casualties
nuclear_setback_assessment: Nuclear posture raised; strategic pressure on Russia increased

Israel did not comment, but sources later indicated the operation was enabled by Israeli satellite and drone surveillance. This was Israel's first strike on Iran's main diplomatic mission in Damascus, significantly escalating Tehran's war posture.

Israeli air defenses intercepted most threats; only light damage and one injury were reported (a 7-year-old girl). Iran said the attack was retaliation for an Israeli strike on its Damascus consulate. incident_title: LUCAS Combat Debut: First US One-Way Attack Drones vs. Iran
origin_label: US Army / CENTCOM AORR (Kuwait / Middle East)
origin_sublabel: Task Force Scorpion Strike — under SOCCENT
target_label: Iran — IRGC Command Nodes + Air Defense + Missile Sites
target_sublabel: Multiple fixed military targets across western and central Iran
summary: CENTCOM's Task Force Scorpion Strike (TFSS) deployed the Low-cost Unmanned Combat Attack System (LUCAS) in combat for the first time in history during Operation Epic Fury. Formed in December 2025 under SOCCENT after SecDef Hegseth directed 'Unleashing US Military Drone Dominance' in July 2025, TFSS employed ground-launched LUCAS drones against IRGC command and control nodes, air defense assets, missile launch infrastructure, and military airfields. The LUCAS is a reverse-engineered Shahed-136 built by Arizona-based SpektreWorks — a V-shaped delta-wing loitering munition powered by a DA-215 two-cylinder engine, ~10ft long, 8ft wingspan, ~$35,000 per unit. It features autonomous coordination, Starlink terminals enabling swarm networking, and can be launched via catapult, RATO, or vehicle-based systems. A LUCAS was first ship-launched from USS Santa Barbara (LCS) in December 2025. CENTCOM announced: 'These low-cost drones, modeled after Iran's Shahed drones, are now delivering American-made retribution.' A malfunction video of an early LUCAS launch circulated on social media but was unconfirmed by CENTCOM.
assessment: LUCAS is the US answer to the drone cost-exchange asymmetry that has favored Iran and Russia throughout the 2020s. At $35,000 per unit versus $4M+ for a Patriot PAC-3 interceptor, the US is now fielding mass-scale expendable munitions at cost parity with adversary systems. The deliberate irony of using reverse-engineered Iranian drones against Iran was intentional — a symbolic and strategic message. LUCAS's Starlink-enabled swarm coordination represents a step-change beyond the simple fixed-target Shahed, enabling dynamic targeting and autonomous cooperative strikes. The combat debut 8 months after public unveiling is a compression of the normal US acquisition timeline, signaling a new doctrine of rapid fielding under wartime conditions.
munitions_weapons: LUCAS (Low-cost Unmanned Combat Attack System) — SpektreWorks FLM-136 derivative
range_km: ~800 km (estimated, based on Shahed-136 baseline ~2000 km range)
target_type: IRGC C2 / air defense / missile infrastructure / military airfields
result_outcome: First combat use confirmed — targets struck, BDA classified
economic_impact: Part of Brent +9% spike; broader Epic Fury economic impact
key_intelligence_notes: Reverse-engineered from captured Iranian Shahed-136; MUSIC mesh network enables swarm ops; Starlink terminals for dynamic targeting; Dec 2025 ship-launch test from USS Santa Barbara (LCS)
osint_sources: The War Zone (TFSS creation Dec 2025); DefenseScoop (LUCAS remains ready Mar 2026)

incident_title: LUCAS Ship-Launch Test: USS Santa Barbara (LCS)
origin_sublabel: CENTCOM AOR — first ship-based LUCAS test
target_sublabel: Two weeks after TFSS announced; first naval LUCAS fire
summary: Two weeks after CENTCOM announced Task Force Scorpion Strike, US Navy personnel test-fired a LUCAS drone from the Independence-class Littoral Combat Ship USS Santa Barbara (LCS-32) in the Persian Gulf — the first ship-based launch of the system. CENTCOM Commander Adm. Brad Cooper posted 'Bravo Zulu' on social media confirming the test. The launch demonstrated the potential for LCS vessels to serve as LUCAS motherships, extending LUCAS's reach into the maritime domain and setting conditions for sea-based one-way attack drone operations alongside Task Force 59 (maritime drone task force).
assessment: The combination of Task Force Scorpion Strike (land-based LUCAS) and Task Force 59 (sea-based autonomous systems) indicates CENTCOM is building an integrated, multi-domain drone strike network across the region. LCS vessels, previously criticized for limited combat utility, may find a new role as LUCAS launch platforms. Ship-based LUCAS deployment would enable strikes from sea approaches not covered by ground basing — significantly complicating Iranian targeting of launch sites.
platform_or_unit: Task Force Scorpion Strike + US Navy (CENTCOM AOR)
result_outcome: Test successful

incident_title: IDF Offensive Campaign: Hezbollah Enters War (Op. Roaring Lion Northern Front)
origin_sublabel: Ramat David + multiple IAF bases; northern ground forces
target_sublabel: Hezbollah military infrastructure, C2, communications, Radwan Force
summary: Hezbollah broke the November 2024 ceasefire on March 1–2, 2026, firing missiles and drones at Israeli bases — Ramat David airbase, Camp Yitzhak, and the Meron monitoring base — declaring it was 'revenge for the blood of Supreme Leader Khamenei.' It was the first Hezbollah cross-border attack since the 2024 ceasefire. Israel responded with an immediate IDF offensive campaign across Lebanon. Within 48 hours: dozens of strikes hit Dahiyeh (south Beirut Hezbollah stronghold), southern Lebanon, and the Beqaa Valley. Hezbollah intelligence chief Hussein Makled was killed overnight Mar 2. Al-Manar TV headquarters and al-Nour radio station were struck. Israel issued evacuation orders to 50+ villages and 59 areas including Dahiyeh neighborhoods. IDF ground forces pushed into southern Lebanon in a new incursion on Mar 3, seizing 'strategic areas' and commanding high ground. Lebanon's army pulled back from at least 7 border positions. 30,000+ displaced. IDF Chief of Staff Zamir declared: 'We have begun an offensive campaign against Hezbollah. We are not just on defense, now we go on the offense.' Netanyahu authorized IDF to 'advance and take control of additional commanding areas in Lebanon.' Israel is calling up tens of thousands of reservists near the Lebanese border, fueling speculation of deeper ground invasion.
assessment: Hezbollah entered the war significantly weakened from the 2024 conflict — IDF had already eliminated 267+ operatives during the ceasefire period. Naim Qassem declined to join the June 2025 12-day war; his decision to enter in March 2026 reflects Iranian pressure post-Khamenei assassination. The IDF ground incursion follows a familiar pattern: use air strikes to suppress air defense and communications, then advance ground forces to buffer zone positions. Lebanon's government banning Hezbollah's military activities signals political fracture within Lebanon but Hezbollah dismissed it. The dual threat to Israel (Iran campaign + Lebanon front) is exactly the multi-front scenario the IDF had been planning for under Op. Roaring Lion. IDF COS Zamir confirmed: 'The IDF planned and is prepared to operate in multiple arenas simultaneously.'

incident_title: Targeted Strike: Hezbollah Intelligence Chief Hussein Makled Killed
origin_sublabel: Precision airstrike on senior Hezbollah leadership
target_label: Beirut area — Hezbollah intelligence HQ location
summary: Overnight March 2–3, 2026, Israel conducted a precision strike in the Beirut area killing Hussein Makled, identified by the IDF as the head of Hezbollah's intelligence headquarters. The IDF stated Makled was responsible for 'forming the intelligence picture using various intelligence collection tools to provide the Hezbollah terror organization with intelligence assessments regarding IDF troops and the State of Israel.' Simultaneously, the IDF struck a key Radwan Force operative in southern Lebanon. Defense Minister Katz publicly declared Hezbollah chief Naim Qassem 'a marked target for elimination.' Reports also indicated strikes were targeting Naim Qassem himself and Mohammad Raad (head of Hezbollah's parliamentary bloc) — Raad's whereabouts were unknown and his body was being searched for in rubble per Al Hadath.
assessment: Makled's elimination degrades Hezbollah's real-time intelligence on IDF movements — critical as Israel launches its ground incursion into southern Lebanon. Targeting the intelligence chief in the opening hours of an offensive is a deliberate pattern (see: 12-Day War opening salvo eliminating 40 IRGC commanders). Katz's public designation of Qassem as a 'marked target' signals an impending leadership decapitation attempt, following the same Roaring Lion doctrine applied to Khamenei. The simultaneous pursuit of Raad (political-military figure) suggests Israel is pursuing both military and political-military Hezbollah leadership simultaneously.

incident_title: IDF Pre-War Hezbollah Degradation: Nov 2024–Feb 2026 Ceasefire Strikes
target_label: Southern Lebanon + Beqaa Valley + North of Litani
target_sublabel: Hezbollah missile array, Radwan Unit, engineering infrastructure
summary: During the November 2024–February 2026 ceasefire period, the IDF continued targeted strikes against Hezbollah across Lebanon on a near-daily basis, citing Hezbollah's failure to disarm and withdraw per the ceasefire terms. By the time Hezbollah entered the war in March 2026, the IDF had: eliminated 267+ Hezbollah operatives (confirmed via open source); killed 20 operatives in February 2026 alone (44 total strikes that month, a 50% decrease from January's 88 strikes); destroyed engineering vehicles used to rehabilitate terror infrastructure; targeted weapons depots in civilian areas (with advance warnings); struck rocket launchers and Radwan Unit camps; and maintained 5 border positions in Lebanese territory. February 2026 strikes included killing 8 Hezbollah missile force operatives in Beqaa Valley, including a 'commander' — one of the deadliest ceasefire-period strikes. Strike geography: south of Litani, north of Litani (increasing), and Beqaa Valley (consistent presence).
assessment: The IDF's continuous ceasefire-period degradation represents a sustained attrition campaign that significantly weakened Hezbollah before it re-entered open hostilities. Eliminating 267 operatives over 15 months — including missile array commanders, Radwan Unit officers, air defense personnel, and engineering department heads — systematically degraded Hezbollah's reconstitution effort. This established the conditions for the IDF's rapid offensive campaign response when Hezbollah fired in March 2026. The pattern mirrors IDF doctrine in Gaza: maintain constant pressure even under ceasefire conditions to prevent adversary reconstitution.
munitions_quantity: 267+ operatives KIA across campaign; 44 strikes in Feb 2026 alone
